dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 79 responding for breathing problems in Richmond.
Hi, my name is Jordan and I am an EMT. You seem to be having trouble breathing. What made you call 911?
I can't breathe properly, and it is getting worse.
Let's check your level of alertness. Can you open your eyes and tell me what's going on?
Yes, I can hear you.
Excellent, you're alert and oriented. What's the worst part of your breathing right now?
To be thorough, I'll ask you a few quick questions. First, can you state your name for me?
My name is Edward.
Great. What city are we in?
Richmond.
And the date? Just the month and day.
May 11th.
He started knocking on my door because he was struggling to breathe. He could barely speak through each breath.
Your airway appears clear. I'll need to check your oxygen saturation. That's part of stabilizing your breathing.
His SpO₂ is 57%. That's very low, we need to act quickly.
We can see your breathing is rapid and labored. Are you feeling like you can get a full breath at all?
Very hard. It's just constant.
Respirations are at 30 per minute with significant work and effort.
We heard diminished breath sounds. Is there a feeling of something restricting your chest or lungs?
Yes, I feel like I am fighting each breath.
We'll check your pulse and blood pressure now. These will help us better understand your needs.
Pulse is 99 beats per minute. Strong and thready.
Blood pressure is 234 over 105. That's very high for you.
You show signs of severe respiratory distress, pale and diaphoretic skin. We need to escalate care.
Blood sugar is unable to be obtained, the patient is too diaphoretic for prick method.
Have you had any prior heart or lung conditions before today?
I have a history of congestive heart failure.
Are you taking any daily medications to manage your condition?
I don't take any medication for it.
He knocked on my door in obvious distress and could barely speak at all.
What was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
I had some soup and water about two hours ago.
I'll follow up with the pain assessment. Are you experiencing any chest or body pain at the moment?
No pain at this time.
Given your difficulty breathing, low oxygen, and history of CHF, we are following the pulmonary edema protocol.
We're applying a transdermal nitroglycerin patch to help improve blood flow and ease the work of breathing.
We're setting up CPAP now to support your breathing more effectively.
Your pulse has dropped slightly to 98.
Blood pressure is 236 over 119 - still elevated.
Saturation remains at 57%.
Note that your skin is pale and very sweaty, which is common in patients with distress and hyperperfusion.
Let me do a quick visual check. Are there any bumps, bruises, cuts, or visible lumps on your head or neck?
No, nothing that I'm aware of.
We're not seeing any jugular vein distention, so that helps support that the problem in your chest is more related to heart or lungs than blood return issues.
Let's listen to your lungs. We expect to hear signs of fluid or congestion.
With CPAP and nitro on, we're hearing bilateral crackles - this supports a diagnosis of pulmonary edema.
Initial EKG shows sinus tachycardia. We're keeping an eye on it.
We are doing a 12-lead EKG to check for any cardiac cause behind your increased work of breathing.
The EKG now shows findings consistent with STEMI and possible anterolateral infarct. This supports the need for quick transport for intervention.
We completed the 12-lead and it's being transmitted for review.
Current pulse is 84.
Your breathing has stayed at 30 per minute.
Blood pressure has dropped slightly to 209 over 122.
Oxygen shows a small improvement to 69%.
I'll attach a 4-lead EKG for continuous monitoring during transport.
A second 4-lead EKG for comparison, just to ensure consistency and rule out new changes.
EKG still showing STEMI pattern and ST Elevations consistent with a cardiac event.
Applying third 4-lead EKG just to verify no new changes.
Repeating the 4-lead EKG-still recording the same pattern. No changes.
Continuing 4-lead-no acute new issues observed.
Another 4-lead - double-checking for any subtle changes.
EKG readings still stable for now. No unexpected findings.
Pulse is now 94.
Respirations have decreased slightly to 26 per minute.
Blood pressure now 181 over 100.
Oxygen saturation has worsened to 38%.
Repeating the 4-lead monitoring again for reassurance.
No new acute changes today. Sinus rhythm is still present.
We're transporting you to VCU Health Systems for specialized care, including possible cardiac intervention.
Securing you on a stretcher with a 5-point harness to ensure maximum stabilization during transport.
Glucose still unobtainable due to diaphoresis. No value could be recorded.
Powering on all monitoring equipment to ensure continuous data recording during transport.
All your lines and equipment are in place and secured. We're ready to transport now.
ED team, we have a 65-year-old male with possible STEMI and acute pulmonary edema.
Oxygen saturation at 38%, respiratory rate of 26. We applied a transdermal nitro patch and are on CPAP.
Understand. I'll prepare the cath lab team as we suspect a cardiac event.
12-lead EKG completed and attached to the device for your review.
All monitors are powered and active for your team during the transfer.
We are maintaining three continuous 4-lead EKG lines throughout transport.
Received all information and equipment. Thank you for the documentation.
We have the patient and continuous monitoring in place. Ready for handoff.
We transferred the patient using a sheet lift method to avoid movement that could affect vitals.
